meeting_name: Edge Animal Health & SimplerQMS - Follow Up
pain_points: Edge Animal Health is building a QMS from scratch with a small team and limited capacity, needing fast implementation, ease of use, and strong support without complex IT dependencies or validation burden.
buying_signals: Prospect stated 'We're definitely getting ready to make a final decision and move forward' and 'Everything you've described has met our expectations'; agreement to send contract for $17,888.26 with a follow-up scheduled June 22 and a 1-week internal decision timeline.
risks: Internal alignment still needed on contract length (1-year vs. multi-year) and Shane's role and influence in the final decision remain unclear, representing minor but non-zero delay risk.
next_steps: Send main agreement (10 users, $17,888.26) within 1 hour of call and hold follow-up meeting Monday June 22 at 11 AM ET to finalize contract length and confirm kickoff date.
key_quote: Everything you've described has met our expectations
closure_reason: Edge Animal Health selected SimplerQMS to build their quality management system from the ground up, recognizing that the platform's streamlined implementation could meet their aggressive mid-August launch deadline without requiring extensive resources from their lean team. The buying signals throughout the evaluation made it clear that SimplerQMS delivered on its promises—Emma's feedback confirmed the solution aligned perfectly with what the team needed to achieve rapid validation and get compliant quickly. With a clear path to go-live and confidence in the product's capabilities, Edge Animal Health committed to the $17,826 annual investment to power their QMS launch.

pain_points: Innovia Health is an early-stage healthcare organization moving away from paper-based processes and needs an easy-to-implement, pre-validated eQMS covering document control, training, and compliance without heavy customization or revalidation burden. They are building their QMS from scratch with a small 5-person leadership team wearing multiple hats.
buying_signals: Maude expressed strong enthusiasm for Word integration ('I really love that it's implemented in the Microsoft Word'), confirmed pricing is within budget range, and is setting SimplerQMS as the benchmark for other vendors evaluated. She is the primary decision-maker, asked detailed probing questions throughout, and agreed to a follow-up call in two weeks after looping in her leadership team.
risks: Quiz/test functionality is not yet available (roadmap end of 2026/early 2027), which could be a gap if training assessment is a compliance requirement. Maude still needs to align with her 5-person leadership team before a decision, introducing potential delay or internal pushback.
next_steps: Martin to send collaboration room link (demo recording, slide deck, case studies, validation docs), official quote (~$21,445/year for 5 Full + 15 Light licenses), SLA documentation, and customer references; follow-up call scheduled for 2 weeks out (~8-9 AM). Maude to review with leadership team by end of week.
key_quote: It's clearly within the range that we discussed with my team.

meeting_name: SimplerQMS & Omni Flow Computers - Overview, Demo, and Pricing
pain_points: OmniFlow is on Master Control with expensive on-premise infrastructure (3 servers), rigid life-sciences-specific fields that don't fit their oil & gas workflows, and an offline document editing process that creates unnecessary upload/download cycles. The system is hard to support and misaligned with the team's work style.
buying_signals: Ksenia (primary decision-maker) upgraded her rating from 8/10 to 9/10 after the AI features discussion and expressed willingness to be SimplerQMS's first oil & gas manufacturing customer. Pricing of $31K/year was received positively as better than the Master Control upgrade cost, and a follow-up call was scheduled for June 19th to compare against SoftExpert.
risks: SoftExpert is an active competing vendor and OmniFlow is not yet exclusive, requiring a comparison brief before the next call. The lack of competency assessment/quiz functionality (only 'Read & Understand' available, quizzes on roadmap) is a noted gap that could be a sticking point.
next_steps: SimplerQMS AE to prepare a detailed pricing quote (valid 90 days) and a SimplerQMS vs. SoftExpert comparison brief; follow-up call scheduled for Thursday June 19th at 10:00 AM CT with Ksenia. Also share demo recording, case studies (Cook Medical reference), and document historical ID field requirement for migration planning.
key_quote: "Willing to be first oil & gas manufacturing customer"

pain_points: Kohler Ventures is transitioning from QT9 due to lack of configurability, manual document control workarounds, and bulk publishing delays consuming 1–2 days per change control cycle. Document control, training management, CAPAs, and Jira integration are critical requirements for their ~5,000-document migration.
buying_signals: Brian (primary evaluator with final approval authority) explicitly praised SimplerQMS's Word integration and effective date automation, and confirmed pricing falls within their $50K–$65K annual budget. An internal stakeholder recommended SimplerQMS, and Brian is willing to adjust user licensing to optimize cost, with a decision expected within 2–4 weeks.
risks: Kohler is still in early evaluation with 6 vendors being narrowed to ~3, and no commitment has been made after only 3 weeks into the process. Gaps around parallel multi-reviewer capability and direct SharePoint/Jira integration clarity remain unresolved and could surface as blockers during team demo and sandbox testing.
next_steps: Martin to send collaboration website link with slide deck, demo recording, validation docs, and customer references (immediate). Follow-up email/call later in the week to finalize user licensing breakdown (full vs. standard vs. light). Brian to meet with QA lead to confirm prior SimplerQMS experience and CAD file requirements. Team demo and sandbox testing to follow as vendor list narrows from 6 to ~3.
key_quote: Aligned with regs and business requirements

pain_points: The client (cannabis manufacturer) suffers from fragmented documentation and version control using Box with multi-user conflicts, and relies on manual spreadsheets for batch release and equipment logs with no FDA-compliant audit trail. Approximately 50% of required documentation is still unbuilt, with gaps in training and equipment management.
buying_signals: Grayson confirmed pricing is 'within budget' and committed to a 2-week evaluation period with a follow-up call already scheduled. By end of demo his mental model shifted positively, acknowledging 'it sounds pretty good' and showing high engagement across document control, training, CAPA, and equipment management features.
risks: Grayson is an intermediary consultant and the actual client did not attend, meaning buy-in must be re-established in a second conversation with the end decision-maker. Grayson's self-admitted unfamiliarity with eQMS concepts and need to reconcile the client's existing data structure with SimplerQMS could slow or complicate the evaluation.
next_steps: Grayson to review demo recording, templates, and case studies shared on the collaboration site; follow-up call scheduled for June 23, 2026 at 2 PM (owner: SimplerQMS AE + Grayson) to cover client needs and implementation plan after Grayson consults with the client.

pain_points: Early-stage 4-person medical device startup seeking to move from paper-based systems to digital QMS with integrated PLM, requirements-to-test traceability, and FDA/CFR compliance support. Their primary need for bundled PLM capabilities is not met by SimplerQMS.
buying_signals: Prospect appreciated the product's ease of use and explicitly said 'I may reach out in the future,' leaving the door open for re-engagement in approximately 6 months as the team grows. Rapport with the SimplerQMS rep was strong and Juan was a thorough, engaged evaluator.
risks: Prospect is moving forward with a competitor (TraceX or Open Regulatory) today at roughly an order-of-magnitude lower price point (~$100/month), and SimplerQMS only becomes price-competitive at 10–12 users while the prospect has 4. Migration friction and missing integrated PLM make near-term re-engagement unlikely.
next_steps: Milo to send a brief, friendly (non-sales) check-in email in approximately 3 months to maintain the relationship and stay top of mind for when the team grows.
key_quote: "I may reach out in the future."
loss_reason: Price is considerably higher than competitors for a 4-person team, and SimplerQMS lacks the integrated PLM (requirements → specifications → risk → test traceability) that is the prospect's top priority; competitor chosen today offers both at a fraction of the cost.

pain_points: Roy's QA team advises waiting a year before implementing a QMS, and the €15,000 price point is roughly double the competitor's €7,500 offer, making cost prohibitive for this early-stage 3-person R&D company. Roy is uncertain whether the company will even need a QMS in 12 months given survival risk.
buying_signals: Roy remains open to future conversation if circumstances change and has not yet signed with the competitor, stating he is still evaluating. He appreciated SimplerQMS's document-based flexibility and asked clarifying questions about infrastructure differences.
risks: Roy is leaning strongly toward a cheaper competitor he has prior experience with, and the timing objection is structural rather than tactical—unlikely to resolve in the near term. Even a price match would likely not be sufficient to win the deal at this stage.
next_steps: Milo to send Roy a thank-you note reiterating SimplerQMS's openness to future partnership; Roy to reach out if circumstances change (no firm date set). Milo offered to revisit pricing if Roy shares competitor quote details.
key_quote: "I'm not in the negotiation phase."
loss_reason: Price is 2x the competitor (€15,000 vs €7,500), Roy has prior relationship with competitor, QA team advises waiting a year, and Roy does not believe current stage justifies the investment or premium service tier.

buying_signals: Prospect confirmed SimplerQMS is 'still very much in the running' and praised pricing as 'very reasonable' along with included validation services. pain_points: Enexor Health has no formal QMS procedures in place and minimal regulatory experience, needing to build design controls and FDA 510(k) compliance infrastructure from scratch for a new ventilator. buying_signals: Jake acknowledged SimplerQMS as the 'leading candidate' among three vendors evaluated and expressed genuine interest in its UI, pre-written templates, and scalability for when they are ready to invest. next_steps: Thomas to send collaboration room link (PowerPoint, recording, template index, validation certificates, ROI calculator, pricing) immediately; Thomas to follow up via email in 2–3 months to check design phase progress and budget clarity.

pain_points: Customer needs a QMS for document control and training management in a regulated environment, with plans to expand to change management and quality events. Flexibility and rapid vendor responsiveness were cited as critical requirements.
buying_signals: Pricing of $17,819 annually accepted without negotiation and user count finalized; customer explicitly stated readiness to move forward with a phased implementation plan and contract execution targeted for end of next week.
risks: Executive management review of the contract is the final approval gate, introducing a short delay risk. Minor IT coordination for SSO setup is pending and could affect implementation kickoff timing.
next_steps: Customer (Nicole) to provide contract details (company name, address, invoice contact, approver) and IT contact for SSO by today/tomorrow; Martin (SimplerQMS) to send contract same day; executive review next week; contract execution by end of next week; implementation kickoff targeting July 6th or later.
key_quote: "Huge selling point"; customer appreciates rapid feature iteration.
closure_reason: Skyland Innovation completed their sandbox evaluation by early June and remained actively engaged through the post-evaluation period, demonstrating genuine intent to move forward with SimplerQMS. Nicole's proactive outreach during the check-in phase signaled strong internal buy-in and readiness to implement, which kept momentum on the deal and allowed the team to move toward contract closure. pain_points: Dexio Services is operating on a paper-based QMS with manual document routing, approval workflows, and training record management, creating significant administrative burden as the company scales rapidly. buying_signals: CEO Dave Parker is present, engaged, and driving urgency, with the team accepting sandbox access and committing to test this week followed by a follow-up with Tony next week. risks: Primary decision-maker Tony Price did not attend and authority structure remains unclear, creating a significant stakeholder access risk. Duo MFA integration is unvalidated and could be a non-negotiable deal-breaker; no budget has been discussed and timeline remains vague.

pain_points: Racura is operating manually after exiting their previous eQMS (ACE), with document version control on personal drives, no centralized audit trail, a DocuSign workaround for CFR Part 11 signatures, and no training matrix in place. Inspection readiness and acquisition/diligence exposure add urgency to finding a compliant solution.
buying_signals: Corli self-scored fit at 9/10 and explicitly praised SimplerQMS's Word integration and ease of use, calling it a differentiator from competitors. She engaged deeply in pricing and licensing discussions, asked about phased rollout flexibility, and is actively building the internal business case.
risks: Corli is only 4 months into her role, owns the business case but must present to an exec team and board, and her capacity is split with shifting daily priorities—meaning the follow-up call is unscheduled and prospect-initiated. SimplerQMS is Denmark-based while the prospect flagged an ACE benefit for Australian vendors, which was not addressed during the call.
next_steps: Milo to share presentation, demo recording, and Aligned portal (click-through guides) immediately post-call. Corli to review materials and conduct internal team evaluation; follow-up call to be initiated by Corli based on her capacity—no date set.

pain_points: Kinamed is a medical device manufacturer with ~20 years of paper-based QMS processes (wet signatures, physical binders) facing regulatory pressure from QMSR changes and MDR certification plans, creating urgent need to digitize. Operational overhead from maintaining paper trails and unstructured documentation across decades is a core driver.
buying_signals: Prospect proactively requested a pricing discussion and a follow-up call scheduled for next week, and explicitly acknowledged the system is 'better than all-paper' and 'checks some of our boxes.' Strong engagement from multiple stakeholders (Engineering, Regulatory, Operations) with detailed questions on design control traceability, EU MDR Annex mapping, and audit readiness signals serious evaluation intent.
risks: Prospect explicitly stated they need to 'regroup' and evaluate SimplerQMS against other vendors, indicating active competitive evaluation. Multi-user simultaneous editing is a roadmap gap that engineering flagged, and granular risk-to-complaint reporting is not fully out-of-box, which could become blockers during deeper evaluation.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send recording, prior discussion notes, and platform details by Tuesday May 28; pricing/commercial discussion call scheduled for next week; Kinamed to refine user count estimate (~10 full, 10 standard, ~30 light users).
key_quote: Better than all-paper system, checks some of our boxes.

pain_points: Aroma-Zone currently operates with fragmented quality tools (Monday, Excel, SharePoint, Zendoc) and no centralized QMS, leading to inconsistent execution and compliance risk across their cosmetic and dietary supplement operations. Document migration and integrating ~100–150 users are identified as the most challenging aspects of any transition.
buying_signals: An active vendor evaluation was launched this week, a demo is planned for June/July 2026, and a decision is targeted for end of summer/September 2026, indicating genuine momentum. risks: Budget amount remains unconfirmed and the extended timeline is driven by caution rather than urgency, which could slow or stall the deal. Multiple vendors are being evaluated simultaneously, and unscoped integration requirements (ERP, Freshdesk, Monday) could complicate SimplerQMS's fit assessment.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to complete and return the supplier questionnaire (functionality, pricing, technical specs) and provide a pricing estimate for ~100–150 users; both teams to schedule a 1-hour demo for June or July 2026 post-questionnaire review.

pain_points: The prospect runs a paper-based QMS (~40 SOPs, handwritten records since 2001) with email-routed Word documents where people forget to sign, and lacks centralized tracking for periodic quality activities. They need to modernize to attract new clients, scale headcount, and maintain compliance with Danish Medicines Agency GDP audits every 3 years.
buying_signals: Prospect (Anders) is highly engaged, drove the conversation, acknowledged the ~100k DKK ballpark pricing without objection, and proactively scheduled a demo for June 11. SimplerQMS is ranked #1 among 3–4 vendors being evaluated due to prior familiarity from 2021.
risks: Two critical technical questions remain unresolved heading into the demo: whether SimplerQMS has GDP-specific templates and whether it supports serialization/2D barcode track-and-trace for EU medicine packages. Prospect is at 'square one' and will evaluate 3–4 competitors, so SimplerQMS has no exclusive advantage yet.
next_steps: Demo scheduled June 11 at 2:00 PM (owner: Milo); Milo to send collaboration room link with licensing info beforehand; Milo to confirm GDP-specific templates and serialization capability; prospect to prepare user count and license allocation ideas.

pain_points: Wyss Zurich operates a fully paper-based QMS with manual scanning, printing, and Excel-managed training processes, creating significant QA capacity bottlenecks. They also require strict multi-project document segregation and are concerned about validation complexity when adopting a new eQMS.
buying_signals: Prospects explicitly stated the system 'looks good' and 'simple to use,' and proactively engaged in licensing discussions. Budget is approved, a 12-month migration target is set, and an end-of-summer vendor decision aligns with SimplerQMS's 6–8 week implementation timeline.
risks: University procurement process may introduce delays beyond the prospect's control. SimplerQMS is on a 3-vendor shortlist competing against SciLife and MatrixOne (SimCloud), and quality events (CAPA/deviations) were not fully demoed, leaving a potential gap.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send proposal/pricing before follow-up call (by end of day per notes); prospect to review materials in collaboration/sandbox room; follow-up call scheduled with clear deliverables to deep-dive CAPA and deviations.
key_quote: System looks good and simple to use.

pain_points: NSK is managing quality documentation via Excel lists and intranet folders with paper-based processes, seeking to implement a centralized document control system and transition to fully digital/paperless operations. Secondary needs include CAPAs, audits, and training management.
buying_signals: Budget of $17,500 minimum was confirmed as aligned with market expectations, and Alma agreed to schedule a full demo for the following week (Monday or Tuesday afternoon). Engaged stakeholders with decision-making authority were present and expressed interest in multiple QMS modules.
risks: Authority is only partially confirmed — Alma's boss must be included in future discussions, adding a layer of approval uncertainty. No purchase timeline or urgency signals were identified, and a language barrier (Spanish-speaking team) could slow evaluation and adoption.
next_steps: Martin to send calendar link for 1-hour demo call (Monday or Tuesday afternoon next week); Alma to confirm attendee list including her boss and determine exact user count for pricing; Martin to prepare demo covering document management, training management, complaints, and CAPAs.
key_quote: The cost aligns with comparable systems in the market.

pain_points: AquaBioTech relies entirely on manual, Excel-based QMS with printed wet-signed SOPs and no audit trail, making compliance demonstration difficult ahead of upcoming authority audits. Joseph manages all QA alone with no automated training notifications, forcing supervisors to manually chase staff with paper sheets.
buying_signals: Prospect explicitly stated 'this is something that needs to be done as soon as possible' and called the €15,500 annual pricing 'very reasonable,' proactively offering to send exact user counts by Monday. next_steps: SimplerQMS (Alex) to populate shared workspace with documentation, SLA, pricing, and ROI calculator before May 20; prospect to send exact user counts by Monday; follow-up meeting scheduled for Wednesday May 20 at 2:00 PM CET with finance department potentially joining.

pain_points: Agnes America has 2,000–3,000 documents scattered across Dropbox, Zoho, Monday Boards, and local servers with no validated eQMS, creating serious FDA compliance risk. Manual document management is not scalable and leaves them exposed to potential regulatory warning letters.
buying_signals: Paul called the ~$18,000/year pricing 'reasonable' and Asli confirmed the solution aligns with her priority to 'get everything organized.' Both decision-makers engaged positively with the demo and agreed on a Q1 2027 implementation target with vendor selection before year-end.
risks: Budget approval is contingent on headquarters sign-off with no committed budget yet, and a competing vendor evaluation is still ongoing. The 8-month runway to Q1 2027 creates significant risk of momentum loss and potential displacement by a competitor.
next_steps: Thomas to send collaboration room link with recording, PowerPoint, quote (~$18,000/year), and implementation plan within the hour; Thomas to follow up in ~1 month; Paul & Asli to secure HQ budget approval and evaluate competing vendors before year-end.
key_quote: "get everything organized"

pain_points: Cassini is replacing paper-based QMS processes in a regulated medical device/pharma environment requiring FDA audit readiness. Key functional needs include document control, training management, traceability, and asset/equipment management.
buying_signals: Prospect negotiated pricing to an acceptable range, opted for a 2-year agreement to lock in pricing, and stated readiness to start immediately upon contract signature with CEO sign-off expected imminently. All objections were resolved and no unresolved blockers remain.
risks: The newer SimplerQMS version lacks full asset/equipment management until next year's roadmap delivery, requiring the prospect to use the older version. If the CEO delays signature or raises new concerns during PO review, momentum could stall.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send updated quote (10% year-1 discount + 2-year locked pricing) and agreement with intake questionnaire; Cassini to review with CEO and issue PO; Cassini to send supplier questionnaire — all actions expected to complete within days of this call.
key_quote: Ready to start immediately upon contract; no audit/compliance blockers
closure_reason: Cassini Technologies B.V. closed on SimplerQMS after Vaiva Lialyte's team provided constructive feedback during their post-demo session, demonstrating genuine engagement with the platform's capabilities. Allan Murphy Bruun's proactive participation in the feedback call signaled strong internal buy-in and helped address any remaining implementation concerns, moving the deal forward to closure. The team recognized that SimplerQMS solved their quality management challenges and committed to the $20,270 investment.

pain_points: Brainomix has needs around document control and training management but has decided to address these internally via Jira and Confluence rather than adopting an external eQMS. Ongoing license fees and implementation effort were cited as key concerns driving this decision.
buying_signals: SimplerQMS UI/UX was praised as clean, modern, and intuitive, and Timothy acknowledged SimplerQMS would have been shortlisted under a different strategy. Re-engagement remains conditionally possible if the internal Jira/Confluence approach fails for document control or training.
risks: The strategic decision to avoid external eQMS solutions is firm, with Brainomix committing internal resources to a Jira/Confluence build, making near-term re-engagement very unlikely. If the internal solution succeeds, the opportunity may be permanently lost; if it fails, competitors could also be evaluated.
next_steps: SimplerQMS (Milo) to maintain low-touch nurture and remain available if Brainomix revisits eQMS need; no specific date set. Brainomix (Timothy) to proceed with fleshing out Jira/Confluence integration for document and training management.
key_quote: Clean user interface, quite nice and intuitive, modern interface.
loss_reason: Brainomix made a strategic decision to build internally using Jira and Confluence, citing ongoing license fees as less cost-effective than internal development and preferring to leverage existing tools over adopting a new external eQMS.

pain_points: Magnum Compounding relies on a fragmented mix of paper, Excel, and PMS for training and document control, making audits and team collaboration difficult. They need a lightweight, centralized QMS that is intuitive for non-QA pharmacy staff without enterprise-level complexity.
buying_signals: LT (primary decision-maker) stated 'I think that it does' fit requirements and asked detailed technical questions on field locking, PDF support, and Word versions, indicating genuine evaluation intent. The team agreed to a defined follow-up path including a conditional 2-week trial if SimplerQMS is confirmed as a top vendor candidate.
risks: Pricing came in roughly 40% above LT's initial budget expectation ($100–$1,000/month vs. ~$1,400/month), and ROI justification depends on actual team usage rather than system capability alone. No firm timeline or urgency was expressed, and buy-in from other team members (cameras off) remains unknown.
next_steps: Martin to send presentation slides and pricing details (owner: Martin, immediate); LT to review with team and respond via email on decision to proceed (owner: LT, no firm date given); conditional 2-week trial to be set up by Martin if LT confirms SimplerQMS as a top vendor candidate.
key_quote: I think that it does fit requirements.

pain_points: Charles Watts must rebuild their ISO 9001 documentation system from scratch after a CRM/software transition left all documentation unregulated, while still relying on cumbersome paper-based processes ahead of an upcoming audit.
buying_signals: Clear, urgent need to digitalize ISO 9001 compliance with a defined user count of 4–5 users, and an audit deadline later in the year creating moderate time pressure.
risks: The €15,500 minimum price is 'on the high end' of Jamie's expectations with no formal budget allocated, and no demo or concrete next steps were scheduled by end of call, indicating low near-term conversion probability.
next_steps: Martin to share interactive demo link with Jamie (actioned at 10:12); SimplerQMS rep to follow up with Jamie to clarify budget constraints, identify secondary stakeholders, and confirm audit timeline — no specific date set.

pain_points: BioReference is managing QMS through disparate systems (Excel, Media Lab, MasterControl) with no unified platform, recent inspection findings, and understaffing making manual data management unsustainable. They urgently need a system people will actually adopt that supports compliance, document control, training, and quality events.
buying_signals: Brianna confirmed SimplerQMS is in the final 4 of ~7 vendors evaluated, with 3 already eliminated; Melissa and Aedan gave positive reactions to Word/Excel integration and document-based workflows, and the team confirmed document control and change workflows meet their needs. Brianna proactively requested to see the supplier management module and noted it addresses a current gap.
risks: Brianna rated the platform only 7/10 citing lack of AI-driven analytics as a key limitation, and Aedan gave 7-8/10 with uncertainty; the final decision requires broader team consensus and comparison against 3 remaining competitors. The missing ability to send supplier questionnaires directly from the system and the unclear AI roadmap are unresolved gaps.
next_steps: Brianna to coordinate next steps after audit schedule clears (multiple audits this week and next); Thomas to resend the quote from early April. Owner: Thomas (resend quote immediately); Brianna (follow up post-audit).
key_quote: Brianna: 7/10 limited by lack of AI; Aedan: 7–8/10 potential but uncertain.

next_steps: SimplerQMS team (including co-founder Alan) to visit PSC pavilion at BIO Conference in ~6 weeks; Thomas to leverage BIO partner portal for outreach and meeting scheduling; Tamy and Thomas to formally reconnect post-BIO in June (owner: Thomas Tschirhart).

pain_points: Pyure needs a paperless, digitally integrated eQMS and selected SimplerQMS over Greenlight Guru for those capabilities. They are under internal pressure to move quickly and book implementation resources.
buying_signals: Danny explicitly stated the goal to close 'today or by tomorrow' and Pyure has already identified implementation leads (Herman and Jeff) with a kickoff target of May 18th confirmed. SimplerQMS was chosen over Greenlight Guru, signaling strong vendor commitment.
risks: The absence of a force majeure clause in the SimplerQMS agreement is flagged as high severity and requires Pyure board approval before signing. Contract length (1, 2, or 3 years) remains undecided, which also impacts pricing tier and must be resolved before the agreement can be routed.
next_steps: Pyure (Danny) to present force majeure concern and contract length options to CEO and board, returning a decision by midday tomorrow. SimplerQMS to prepare all three agreement versions (1-, 2-, 3-year) ready for immediate routing upon Pyure's decision.
key_quote: today or by tomorrow
closure_reason: The Pyure Company closed on a $14,500 deal after progressing through agreement review and moving into final contract signing stages with SimplerQMS. The organization had reached an advanced buying stage where they were prepared to formalize their eQMS implementation, indicating strong internal alignment around the solution's value for their life sciences operations. With negotiations finalized and both parties ready to execute, the deal moved from pipeline to closed won.

pain_points: Ocugen needs a standalone document management and training system for a specific site, currently storing documents in SharePoint in a regulated life-sciences environment requiring audit readiness and compliance. They also need scalable training assignment and tracking without burdening staff with excessive onboarding.
buying_signals: The prospect gave explicit positive verbal feedback — 'It's good. It's well integrated. Ease of use is pretty nice' — and engaged with detailed technical questions on training logic, pricing scalability, and SSO integration. They agreed to a follow-up call in the last week of May to continue evaluation after internal review.
risks: Two training-related feature gaps are unresolved: whether training can be skipped for minor/formatting version changes, and whether prior training can carry over to a new minor version — the presenter indicated ~99% certainty the latter is not possible. Pricing requires internal budget approval and the main contact is not the final decision-maker.
next_steps: Arpita to reach out to schedule a follow-up call in the last week of May; SimplerQMS presenter to confirm with product team on training bypass and version credit questions; share collaboration website with recordings, templates, validation docs, and quote.
key_quote: Ease of use is pretty nice. It's more self-explanatory.

pain_points: Calpro is forced to replace their current Formpipe/XDOCS system due to divestiture costs and rigidity, with a hard Q2 2027 go-live deadline. Their small team struggles with validation overhead, system inflexibility, and the need to consolidate QMS across a pending UK merger.
buying_signals: Prospect confirmed pricing is 'reasonable' and willing to 'start with that,' requested SLA and validation SOP documentation, and scheduled a specific follow-up call for June 4, 2026—all strong due-diligence and commitment signals. Kris engaged deeply with technical details across all modules with no disengagement or feature rejections.
risks: Calpro is evaluating 11 vendors simultaneously and Kris noted it is 'too early to say' on overall fit, meaning SimplerQMS must land on the shortlist by end of May to progress. The unresolved segregation-of-duties concern (no built-in lock preventing same person from authoring and approving) is a pending functional gap that needs a confirmed answer from the SimplerQMS team.
next_steps: Alex to send SLA terms, validation SOP, and segregation-of-duties clarification, and create a shared documentation workspace; follow-up call scheduled June 4, 2026 at 9:30 AM (30 min) to confirm shortlist status.

pain_points: Metalion faces critical label/packaging version control risk (50k+ unit batches at risk from wrong labels) and SharePoint-related document overwriting causing significant economic damage. They also need multi-country compliance (ISO 13485 & ISO 9001) across Swiss and German entities with differing e-signature standards.
buying_signals: Prospect explicitly stated 'The answer is yes, we like the product' and 'We like the approach,' and is ready to sign pending a formal offer for 5 full licenses. They discussed a July 1st go-live target and framed urgency as weeks not months.
risks: Microsoft/SSO adoption is unresolved—prospect is hesitant about forcing Microsoft accounts on staff and external suppliers, and Swiss vs. German tenant complexity needs internal IT alignment before implementation. VAT treatment for Swiss entity also requires financial team confirmation.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send company information form immediately after call and deliver formal offer (5 full licenses, Metalion Health AG) by afternoon pending US manager approval; prospect to complete internal homework on Microsoft/SSO and entity clarification.
key_quote: The answer is yes, we like the product
closure_reason: Metalion Health AG decided to move forward with SimplerQMS after a productive post-demo discussion with their 4-person leadership team, including the CEO, who were ready to begin onboarding. The company's executive alignment and immediate transition to implementation indicated strong confidence that SimplerQMS would solve their quality management needs more effectively than their existing processes. The $14,725 contract represented a clear investment in modernizing their eQMS infrastructure for their life sciences operations.

pain_points: Garmin is managing change management (engineering changes, BOMs, tooling, documents) across multiple disconnected systems and paper-based processes, with change orders losing visibility and unclear ownership. They run 2–3 change controls per day with up to 30 active simultaneously under ISO 9001 compliance.
buying_signals: Both stakeholders were actively engaged with detailed technical questions indicating serious evaluation, and the prospect explicitly requested a sandbox trial with their own templates. Flexibility of template customization and Microsoft Office integration resonated strongly.
risks: Two high-severity gaps were identified: the system cannot enforce series-based/sequential action workflows and lacks a built-in manager dashboard for bottleneck visibility by person or department. The prospect also found the system more complex than expected ('neat, quick, punchy') and flagged pricing at ~€1,000/user/year as higher than anticipated, with competing solutions potentially offering missing features.
next_steps: Prospect to conduct internal review of findings; SimplerQMS to set up a sandbox trial with Garmin's own templates and revisit pricing with leadership discussion. Follow-up meeting to discuss internal findings and next steps noted at ~01:10:47.

pain_points: Precision Antibiotics needed a flexible, tag-based QMS suited for a small-to-medium biotech rather than complex enterprise platforms like Veeva or MasterControl. They also required strong data security, clear compliance support, and contractual protections around outsourcing and insolvency.
buying_signals: Jonas accepted the limited-time licensing offer, agreed to a contract signature deadline of Monday, and committed to an in-person onboarding visit for the week of May 18th. He independently verified SimplerQMS with existing customers and explicitly preferred the 'small-to-small interface' over larger platforms.
risks: The liability cap concern raised by Jonas was not fully resolved and remains a potential sticking point with the legal team. A minor T&C typo (Section 14 reference) was flagged for legal review and could delay final signature.
next_steps: Jonas to sign contract by Monday (latest); Alex to coordinate scheduling for Thomas's in-person onboarding visit to Copenhagen area, Tuesday or later the week of May 18th; SimplerQMS legal team to correct T&C Section 14 typo before signature.
key_quote: "small-to-small interface" over larger platforms
closure_reason: Precision Antibiotics ApS closed on SimplerQMS after both parties reached alignment during the contract finalization call, indicating the prospect had resolved any remaining implementation or commercial concerns. The deal value of $13,950 suggests the organization was seeking a scalable quality management solution to streamline their compliance and documentation workflows, which SimplerQMS delivered through its eQMS platform. The swift progression to closing demonstrated strong executive buy-in and confidence that the platform would address their regulatory and operational needs in the life sciences sector.

pain_points: Ozmosis relies on Excel for training records, document version control, audit tracking, and metrics reporting across a 20-year document library. A small QA team of 3–4 staff with no dedicated IT resource makes manual processes increasingly unsustainable as the company grows.
buying_signals: Both attendees asked clarifying questions throughout the demo and reacted positively to the $25K–$35K pricing range, with willingness to provide detailed user counts for a refined quote. Nayab praised ease of learning and Cindy gave constructive feedback, indicating genuine evaluation intent rather than passive interest.
risks: Reporting gaps are a meaningful concern: Cindy rated the solution 7.5/10 due to the lack of automated audit finding categorization and no dedicated SOP deviation module. The prospect is in early comparison-shopping mode ('first demo') with no hard deadline, increasing the risk of competitor displacement or stalled momentum.
next_steps: Martin to: (1) follow up with product team on audit report generation capability and share findings; (2) send collaboration website with recorded call, slide deck, and pricing slides. Ozmosis (Cindy/Nayab) to provide updated user counts so Martin can refine the pricing quote in the collaboration website. Follow-up call to be scheduled as needed.

pain_points: Pro Farm is struggling with paper-heavy workflows and a rigid legacy QMS (Loyal Solutions) that no longer fits their processes after becoming an independent company. Key pain points include lack of template version control, complex training competency verification needs, and EPA external approval tracking.
buying_signals: Tamara engaged with detailed, process-specific questions throughout the demo and pricing was accepted as within expectations at $48,610/year against a $50K–$60K budget. The team agreed to internal evaluation and NDA review, signaling genuine forward momentum.
risks: Pro Farm is actively evaluating multiple vendors and no IT/technical stakeholder was present, meaning integration and validation questions may surface later. Two partially unresolved gaps—lab data collection audit trail and EPA external approval routing—could become blockers if competitors offer native solutions.
next_steps: SimplerQMS (Martin) to send recording, documentation, and collaboration room link by end of day; send follow-up email clarifying date format customization; prepare custom video on training workflow and lab data collection. Pro Farm (Tamara) to meet internally, evaluate alternatives, and engage legal on NDA if proceeding.

pain_points: Stack Clinical struggles with rigid, resource-intensive QMS systems like MasterControl and Trackwise, and needs a low-cost, easy-to-implement solution to manage 180+ SOPs and track training across distributed teams. They also need a partner-friendly QMS they can recommend to their small/mid-sized biotech clients at the CMC/manufacturing stage.
buying_signals: Brian explicitly stated 'You're in the right. There's no doubt about that.' and confirmed they will provide a yay-or-nay decision; pricing of $17,800/year was deemed acceptable and the partner/reseller model was validated early in the call. Both Brian and Heidi affirmed alignment with SimplerQMS's simplicity philosophy and appreciated transparency on roadmap and AI strategy.
risks: Implementation is intentionally deferred to August–September, creating a long sales cycle with potential for competitor re-engagement or internal reprioritization. The document-based deviation/CAPA reporting approach was only cautiously accepted, and Heidi flagged export/audit trail capabilities as an open follow-up question that remains unresolved.
next_steps: Thomas to send collaboration room link with recording, roadmap, SLA details, and 80-templates list (owner: Thomas, immediate). Reconnect call scheduled for week of May 19 for decision update. Heidi to clarify export/audit trail capabilities via follow-up. Brian and Heidi to review materials internally before decision expected late May/early June.

pain_points: The end customer is a small company transitioning to medical device manufacturing needing to replace a deficient ISO 9001 QMS with ISO 13485, with a team that has limited regulatory knowledge and no dedicated QA resource. They need to reduce manual work and are planning to migrate from an e-paper QMS after their September 2026 certification audit.
buying_signals: Valentin asked 15+ detailed technical questions and praised SimplerQMS as the 'clearest quotation system I've been seeing so far,' indicating strong engagement. ISS AG described SimplerQMS as 'potentially a good fit' and committed to presenting it alongside 3 other vendors to the end customer in approximately 2 weeks.
risks: The end customer has not yet committed to buying an eQMS and no decision is expected before end of May, with a realistic full rollout only in Q4 2026. The customer lacks a dedicated QA resource and implementation depends on hiring, which could delay or block the deal entirely.
next_steps: Milo to send follow-up email with collaboration room link, meeting recording, presentation, license breakdown, annual service costs, and Trust Center docs; update pricing slide for 10-user scenario (5 full + 5 standard licenses); add customer journey implementation video to collaboration room; schedule follow-up call Wednesday June 3rd at 9:30 AM; Valentin to present SimplerQMS to end customer alongside 3 other vendors in ~2 weeks.

pain_points: Epione currently relies on physical complaint folders, Excel-based product tracking, and manual SOP management across multiple languages, creating clear compliance and efficiency gaps. They need document control, training, complaints handling, and batch records for MDR-compliant pharmaceutical distribution.
buying_signals: Strong positive feedback on pricing and presentation, deep technical engagement from both attendees with probing questions during the live demo, and a follow-up call scheduled within 1–2 weeks with a Head of QA engagement offered. The prospect expressed strong interest in the complaint module and training workflows after seeing practical solutions demonstrated.
risks: API integration with Business Central is a critical unresolved blocker — Epione needs integrations within 6 months post-onboarding but the new SimplerQMS platform is unlikely to support this before 2027. The prospect rejected the M-Files interim solution due to UI/UX concerns, and key decision-makers (management, Head of QA) were not present on the call.
next_steps: Mohammed to consult management on integration timeline and phase 2 strategy; SimplerQMS to share meeting recording in Align room; follow-up call scheduled in 1–2 weeks including a dedicated session with the Head of QA (owner: Martin/Alan, ~week of May 18 2026).

pain_points: MPP Group is transitioning from paper-based processes post-acquisition with a single QA person managing all compliance, facing cleanroom constraints (expensive pens, paper impracticality) and labor-intensive manual change control. They need digitized batch records with role-based workflows across 20+ product variations but lack ERP visibility for job feasibility.
buying_signals: Eileen was highly engaged, asked detailed technical questions about workflow and permissions, and is preparing to advocate internally to new owners next week. Pricing increase from $15K to $17K was acknowledged without objection, and the company previously evaluated SimplerQMS with existing presentation materials on hand.
risks: Eileen is not the final decision-maker — new owners control budget and strategy, with escrow delays adding funding uncertainty. New owners may prioritize ISO 13485/9001 certification over QMS implementation, and a competing all-in-one solution (QT9 ERP+QMS) is being considered if ERP integration becomes critical.
next_steps: Thomas to send collaboration room link (PowerPoints, videos, product tours) for Eileen's self-guided exploration; follow-up demo/deep dive to be scheduled pending outcome of Eileen's internal meeting with new owners (next week).

pain_points: Blair Image has no QMS system, relying on Excel and email for manual tracking of reviews, approvals, and follow-ups with no centralized audit trail. Remote field service teams cannot log issues efficiently, creating compliance gaps and human error risks.
buying_signals: Prospect showed strong positive reactions to pre-configured dashboards, training automation features, and the Word-based document approach, noting the 6–8 week implementation timeline fits their Q3 target. However, these signals were ultimately overridden by Stephanie's explicit rejection of SimplerQMS as the solution path at close.
risks: Stephanie explicitly stated 'I don't think this is the avenue that we are looking to go into,' signaling a no-go decision driven by a fundamental mismatch between her preference for form-driven data entry and SimplerQMS's document-centric design. Additional unresolved risks include Postgres SQL integration compatibility and a missing quiz/test feature not available until end of 2026.
next_steps: Martin to send technical response on Postgres SQL integration compatibility within 2 business days; optional soft follow-up with Stephanie in 2–3 weeks to share quiz/test roadmap update, but no next steps were agreed on the call.

pain_points: Hameln RDS is a pharma/biotech company (~100-150 employees) currently running a paper-based QMS with Excel tracking and urgently needs to digitize to meet compliance requirements. Key process areas include document management, training, deviations, and OOS workflows.
buying_signals: Martin acknowledged SimplerQMS is better than what is currently being implemented and responded positively to the flat licensing model, fast 6-8 week implementation, and significantly lower cost versus Trackwise/MasterControl. He agreed to receive slides, pricing summary, recording, and sandbox access as follow-up.
risks: Critical functional gaps exist around multi-stage sequential OOS approval workflows and field-level signature placement, with the prospect explicitly stating the current design 'won't work' for complex OOS processes. The final decision-maker (CQO) was absent, Martin is only a recommender with moderate confidence in internal approval, and the €64K price is 3x the competing custom-build estimate.
next_steps: SimplerQMS (Martin Cholesky) to send presentation slides, approximate pricing summary, meeting recording, and collaboration room/sandbox access to Martin at Hameln RDS promptly; CQO engagement needed as next milestone (CQO currently busy with audit).
key_quote: "Won't work" for complex multi-part OOS processes.

pain_points: Biomedica relies on manual, email-based document approval workflows with wet signatures and multiple disconnected tools (DocuSign, Adobe Sign, Excel), with no centralized training records or audit-ready compliance tracking across geographically dispersed teams.
buying_signals: Both Nadina and Ivo engaged deeply—Nadina confirmed pricing is 'manageable' and 'normal to ask for such a price,' both stakeholders confirmed willingness to present to management, and they acknowledged SimplerQMS would eliminate their planned Copilot agent project saving budget. Ivo proactively raised technical questions (SSO, data retention, export) signaling IT buy-in.
risks: Competitive evaluation is actively ongoing with ZenQMS, Veeva, and QT9 demos scheduled, creating real displacement risk. Internal approval timelines are extended by Nadina's vacation (May 1–10) and Bulgarian public holidays, delaying a decision by 10–14 days.
next_steps: SimplerQMS (Milo) to send follow-up email with proposal, validation docs, SLAs, and certifications; prospect to review requirements list and share with quality director; follow-up call scheduled for May 19th at 12:30 PM Bulgaria time.
key_quote: "Manageable" and "normal to ask for such a price."
closure_reason: Biomedical Bonding closed on SimplerQMS because the organization needed a modern eQMS solution to streamline their quality management processes as a growing biotech operation. The two key stakeholders who attended the full product demonstration saw clear value in SimplerQMS's capabilities and recognized it would improve their compliance documentation and operational efficiency. The straightforward buying committee and positive reception during the demo enabled a swift decision to move forward with the $13,500 annual contract.

pain_points: Zep is under FDA remediation from COVID-era compliance failures and struggles with manual batch record errors including handwriting issues, calculation mistakes, and lack of sequential step enforcement. Their core need is a full Electronic Batch Record execution system with automated calculations and operator guidance, not a document-centric eQMS.
buying_signals: Prospect came highly prepared with physical batch records and demonstrated deep understanding of their regulatory landscape. However, no pricing, trial, or follow-up meeting was discussed, and the vendor explicitly disqualified the opportunity.
risks: Fundamental product-market misalignment: SimplerQMS cannot support EBR execution, automated calculations, or role-based process gating that are critical requirements for Zep. The call ended with the SimplerQMS rep disqualifying the opportunity and offering to refer EBR-focused alternatives.
next_steps: Martin (SimplerQMS) to email alternative EBR-focused vendor recommendations to prospect; prospect to explore larger pharma EBR solutions — no follow-up sales meeting scheduled.
key_quote: Prospect needs a system that drives the batch process operationally.
loss_reason: Fundamental product-market misalignment: prospect requires a full EBR execution engine with automated calculations, step-by-step operator guidance, and role-based gating; SimplerQMS is a document-centric eQMS that does not support these capabilities. Vendor self-disqualified and referred prospect to alternative solutions.

pain_points: Horizen Medical is a startup women's health clinic relying on manual processes and uncontrolled Google Docs/SharePoint storage, with no formal QMS despite upcoming OSHA audits and a physical clinic opening in Cincinnati in late May/June 2026. Core gaps include document control, training tracking, and incident/complaint management as they scale to multiple locations.
buying_signals: Soraya, the CCO, proactively researched SimplerQMS and values its out-of-the-box approach, Microsoft Office integration, and multi-site scalability. She acknowledged that poor QMS setup early will cause 'pain later' and agreed to discuss budget and priorities with her husband over the weekend.
risks: Significant budget misalignment: Soraya's expectation is below $5,000/year while SimplerQMS minimum is $17,000/year, and a competitor (HealthStream) has already quoted under $10,000. The husband (final decision-maker) views document management as a 'nice to have,' creating internal resistance that Soraya may not be able to overcome.
next_steps: Martin to send follow-up email with pricing options (4 editing/10 total users and 20-user scenarios) and discuss flexibility; Soraya to discuss budget with husband over the weekend; schedule next call for full demo and complaint management module overview.

pain_points: Dan's consultancy clients hit workflow and training-visibility ceilings on Atlassian/Confluence, and new AI/AVT regulations are forcing rapid QMS implementation for small-to-mid health tech and medical device orgs. However, most of Dan's current clients are 10–40 FTE, making SimplerQMS's €15.5k/year minimum a significant barrier for the bulk of his pipeline.
buying_signals: Dan praised the UI, training analytics, and Word integration ('Looks good'), asked detailed technical questions about API integrations, Google Workspace, and workflow automation, and explicitly wants SimplerQMS 'in the back of my mind for pipeline for us in the next sort of 12 months'. He is open to recommending SimplerQMS to mid-market clients as they scale beyond Atlassian.
risks: Dan stated he is 'not in a huge rush' and the engagement is purely exploratory with no immediate client leads identified, creating a long and uncertain nurture cycle. The €15.5k/year floor rules out the majority of his SMB client base, and product gaps (missing CAPA module, flat folder structure, no nested design history file support) could disqualify SimplerQMS for more complex engagements.
next_steps: Milo to: (1) create a collaboration/shared workspace room with product updates for Dan; (2) send meeting recording, click-throughs, and Trust Center docs; (3) document API integration requirements for calibration logs and complaint handling. risks: Legal review could introduce unexpected requirements or delays with no mitigation plan discussed. A price increase beyond the approved $41,848 budget (to $44,902) required CFO re-approval, and the light license count volatility was acknowledged but not fully resolved.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send updated 5-year agreement (locked at $44,902 with 3 extra licenses waived) and intake questionnaire; InterMed (Amanda/Kevin) to route agreement to CFO and legal, onboard SimplerQMS as supplier, and complete intake questionnaire; both parties to reconnect first week of May to address legal feedback and finalize.
key_quote: InterMed selected SimplerQMS as #1 choice, evaluated twice, same result.
closure_reason: The InterMed Group closed on SimplerQMS after demonstrating strong internal alignment, with both Allan and Martin from their organization actively engaged through the sales process and prepared to move forward. The deal progressed quickly once the product demonstration clearly addressed their core need for streamlined post-demo workflow management, bringing them from consideration directly to commitment. With two key stakeholders aligned and ready to implement, The InterMed Group finalized the $41,848.06 contract to modernize their quality management operations.

pain_points: BRUSS relies on manual Excel-based training tracking, intranet document management, and paper/email-driven change approvals across ~2,000 employees in multiple plants. Auditors require proof of training completion and multi-stakeholder change approvals are slow and unstructured.
buying_signals: Prospect explicitly praised the Microsoft Word integration as a major advantage and showed strong positive reaction to change management workflow automation mapping directly to their current process. Budget is flexible with no hard cap mentioned, and multiple stakeholders engaged deeply throughout the demo.
risks: The flat tag-based folder structure in SimplerQMS does not match BRUSS's required multi-level group/plant/product document hierarchy, which the prospect flagged as a critical concern. Additionally, SimplerQMS only has ISO 9001 templates while BRUSS requires IATF 16949, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, and TISAX coverage, and the key decision-maker Günther left early and did not see the full demo or pricing discussion.
next_steps: SimplerQMS (Milo) to send follow-up email with collaboration room link including all materials, demo recording, and quotation; schedule technical deep dive on folder hierarchy feasibility; ensure Günther reviews materials and pricing before next conversation — no hard date stated.
key_quote: completely different to what we have now

pain_points: Bionix is driven primarily by cost (Master Control costs nearly doubled from original contract) and system complexity—Master Control is seen as overly complicated for a 60–65 person company, with poor adoption after 3–4 years and a frustrating search experience. buying_signals: Prospect stated 'I like everything I'm seeing' and explicitly requested a sandbox trial to test before deciding, with the VP of Operations already endorsing alternatives. The prospect is still completing demos with other vendors (~30 days) and will shortlist 2–3 before a final decision, leaving the outcome uncertain.
next_steps: Martin to send post-call package (presentation, recording, validation docs, interactive demo, quote) immediately; SimplerQMS to prepare sandbox/trial environment within 1–2 weeks; Bionix to complete remaining vendor demos (~30 days) then select top 2–3; follow-up meeting tentatively in ~1 month (Martin to send meeting link).

pain_points: Current QMS (Enzyme) is being sunsetted due to a vendor acquisition, forcing an urgent replacement. Integration with Jira/GitHub causes significant operational friction, and software engineers resist adopting any QMS that pulls them away from their existing tools.
buying_signals: Barb stated 'I haven't heard anything that is turning me off yet' and showed strong interest in AI capabilities, audit readiness, and the no-mandatory-integration model. risks: No pre-assigned budget exists and final approval rests with CEO/COO who are unlikely to join the next call. The sunset date for the current QMS is unresolved and Barb is nervous about the planning window, which could compress the sales cycle or push her toward a piecemeal stopgap solution.

pain_points: Rotor Clip lacks system-to-system integration between their eQMS and Infor ERP, currently relying on manual processes to flow control plans, PFMEA, and process flow diagrams to work orders. The eQMS evaluation is deprioritized due to competing internal projects and limited bandwidth.
buying_signals: Peter is conducting a structured 4-vendor evaluation and is willing to schedule Round 2 follow-up calls, with SimplerQMS still in contention. The owner showed interest in SimplerQMS's document-centric approach and Peter is open to deeper demos with the quality team if SimplerQMS advances to the top 2.
risks: The absence of a native Infor ERP integration is a critical blocker requiring owner approval, and SimplerQMS would need custom work to address it. next_steps: Martin to investigate Infor ERP integration feasibility and prepare an integration roadmap (Day 1 manual → Year 2 automated) and video demos of control plan workflows; Peter to schedule Round 2 follow-up calls week of April 7–11 or later and involve quality team if SimplerQMS reaches top 2.

risks: CuriRX is actively evaluating 2-3 competing vendors with no firm commitment until that process concludes. next_steps: SimplerQMS team to verify multi-axis permission control functionality and clarify document consistency flagging limitations, then share quote and recording via collaboration website; CuriRX to complete remaining 1-2 vendor demos scheduled for next week before making a final decision.

buying_signals: Mark is actively evaluating QMS solutions including MasterControl, has a defined two-phase roadmap (basic QMS in 2026, paperless routers in 2027), and pricing was presented and received without immediate rejection. next_steps: Schedule follow-up call (owner: SimplerQMS AE) to address Mark's differentiation concerns and clarify Word-based vs. configurable platform positioning; send recording and demo materials to Mark for team and executive review (already actioned per notes at 04:52).

buying_signals: SimplerQMS is top of the prospect's shortlist alongside only one other vendor, and price is not a concern. Oleksandr explicitly appreciated the training-completion-before-release feature and confirmed intent to escalate if tasks are done by July.
risks: Transition may slip a full year if process cleanup and EBMR fixes are not completed by July. Company is also under financial pressure with production ramping while sales lag, limiting appetite for new expenditure.
next_steps: Milo to schedule a follow-up meeting with Oleksandr in July to reassess QMS transition readiness and discuss implementation/migration timeline (owner: Milo; target: July 2025).

pain_points: Calea is transitioning from no QMS to a cloud-based system for medical device distribution and retail pharmacy, driven by Ontario privacy regulations requiring Canadian data hosting for provincial government contracts. Their approved budget of ~60K CAD annually may be insufficient for SimplerQMS pricing, and they are uncertain whether they need a full-featured QMS or a simpler platform.
buying_signals: The evaluation is narrowed to a short list of vendors and Savreen committed to providing a final response by end of the week of March 24, 2026, indicating active decision-making. They have a clear go-live target of end of summer 2026 and are engaged enough to discuss user breakdowns and contract terms in detail.
risks: SimplerQMS does not currently host data in Canada, which may be a hard blocker due to Ontario privacy regulations for government contracts, giving competitor Viva/C-15 a structural advantage. The ~60K CAD (~45K USD) budget may fall short of SimplerQMS pricing, and leadership/CEO/CFO approval is still pending.
next_steps: Savreen to confirm user breakdown (est. 400 light users plus full/standard split) and provide final response by end of week of March 24, 2026; SimplerQMS team to resolve Canadian hosting question and prepare a detailed final proposal/quote if hosting and pricing issues can be addressed.
key_quote: Evaluation is narrowed down to a short list of vendors.
loss_reason: No Canadian data hosting available (hard regulatory requirement for government contracts) and budget likely insufficient at ~60K CAD annually.

pain_points: Prospect is transitioning from manual Windows Explorer-based document management to a formal QMS, needing version control, audit trails, CAPA, complaint forms, and CFR Part 11 compliance. Key priorities are ease of use and minimizing training burden for employees.
buying_signals: Prospect aims to complete evaluation and make a final decision by the following week, and gave a positive initial impression noting SimplerQMS could be suitable. Pricing has already been discussed and a license structure (16 licenses: 6 full, 10 light) is in place.
risks: Multiple vendors are being evaluated and the prospect noted differences are 'mostly optical with some handling differences,' indicating no strong differentiation yet. Lisa's absence next week may delay the decision timeline beyond the stated deadline.
next_steps: Martin (SimplerQMS) to follow up next week to check on decision status; send SLA documentation with uptime/time guarantee details to prospect.
key_quote: Differences with competitors are noted as mostly optical with some handling differences.
closure_reason: Zedira GmbH selected SimplerQMS after evaluating the platform through their partner qemicon, which provided credibility and reduced implementation risk for the German biotech firm. The deal closed with strong internal alignment, as four stakeholders from Zedira were engaged in the evaluation process and Martin Hils championed the solution within the organization. SimplerQMS's eQMS capabilities addressed Zedira's need for a compliant, streamlined quality management system that fit their biotech operations and budget of $17,700.

pain_points: Rebecca's company finds MasterControl too expensive and overly complex for their 18-person life sciences firm, with additional modules being cost-prohibitive and requiring separate validation charges. They need deviations, CAPAs, and robust training enforcement beyond what they currently use.
buying_signals: Decision expected by end of month with Rebecca as the primary decision-maker actively comparing platforms around the $20K/year price point. SimplerQMS pricing came in at ~$20,668 annually, aligning well with competitor benchmarks she cited, and she requested a detailed follow-up email with all pricing and platform information.
risks: Several missing features were identified—training gates/prerequisites, controlled copies, full-text document search, and daily training task limits—which are core to Rebecca's current workflow and could be blockers. Rebecca is also evaluating whether to simply absorb MasterControl in-house rather than switching vendors.
next_steps: Martin to send follow-up email with all pricing and platform information (owner: Martin, immediate); Rebecca to review training capabilities and compare findings against current structure before reconnecting (owner: Rebecca, by end of month).

pain_points: Koru Medical is entirely paper-based for manufacturing documentation, DHR, and equipment tracking, making production records labor-intensive and historical data retrieval difficult. They want to go paperless to improve efficiency, enable data trending, and enforce training/calibration controls.
buying_signals: Budget has been allocated this year to revisit the QMS project, and they are actively evaluating vendors with a preliminary quote requested for delivery by the next day. They are considering a phased implementation approach with SimplerQMS as the first step, indicating genuine purchase intent.
risks: SimplerQMS cannot fully meet their advanced requirements around field-level validation, blocking untrained personnel from executing tasks, and preventing selection of out-of-calibration equipment—capabilities they explicitly need. The existence of two product versions (old vs. new still in development) raises concerns about long-term support and product roadmap uncertainty.
next_steps: SimplerQMS to send a preliminary quote by tomorrow and provide work instructions/guided articles for self-study; Koru internal team (Bo, Marlon, Doug, operations and quality) to discuss fit and determine whether a phased approach works; potential sandbox/trial if SimplerQMS advances to final selection.
key_quote: Evaluation of whether SimpleQMS meets original vision or if multiple-step approach is needed

pain_points: The client previously lost data with an improperly backed-up eQMS and needs robust backup and offline access; Kylee is the sole quality resource, creating a bottleneck and driving the need for AI-assisted, easy-to-use document-centric QMS.
buying_signals: Kylee ranked SimplerQMS at the top of her evaluation list due to its document-centric approach, and she appreciated the all-inclusive annual subscription model with no separate implementation fees. The client has a hard deadline of June/July for go-live and a final decision expected within one month.
risks: Kylee is a consultant evaluating five competing platforms (Qualio, Greenlight Guru, Arena QMS, Dock Compliance, ClearMark) and must present findings to the actual decision-maker client, adding an extra layer of influence risk. The client is on spring break the following week, compressing the review timeline.
next_steps: Martin to create and send a collaboration room with demo recording, case studies, and interactive demo materials; Kylee to share with her client; follow-up call scheduled for the week after spring break (approximately 2 weeks out). Client to make final decision within one month.
key_quote: SimplerQMS is ranked at the top by Kylee from a user perspective.

pain_points: The prospect struggles with process gaps in change management and compliance due to the volume of items to track, and has been frustrated by overly complex enterprise EQMS systems in the past. They need a right-sized solution covering document management, training, and non-conformance management.
buying_signals: Prospect assessed the $17,828 annual price as 'reasonable and well within expected range,' and expressed strong satisfaction with SimplerQMS's design and simplicity. They have a clear decision deadline of early June and are actively planning a follow-up demo with their technical writer and CEO stakeholder in mid-April.
risks: Multiple competitors including MasterControl and Viva are being evaluated, and the technical writer and CEO have not yet seen the platform. The deal could stall if internal stakeholders are not engaged before the June decision deadline.
next_steps: Martin to send slide deck and build a collaboration room (with recording and interactive demo) for Jaimie to share internally; schedule follow-up demo with technical writer and team for mid-April; Martin to follow up in 2-3 weeks.
key_quote: Reasonable price, well within expected range.

pain_points: The company's QMS is out of control using a Google Drive folder system, with too many activities (CLIA lab, clinical trial, pharma outsourcing) for a 10-person team. Document management and training management are the top priorities, with compliance gaps in CAPA and non-conformance reporting.
buying_signals: Anker appreciated the document versioning and author tracking features during the demo, and acknowledged SimplerQMS is simpler and more affordable than MasterControl. He committed to presenting SimplerQMS findings to his client and will reach out once budget is assessed.
risks: No budget has been allocated and pricing sensitivity is high at €15,500/year for a small startup; budget rejection is cited as a critical decision factor. Anker is evaluating three competing QMS solutions and there is no defined timeline, making this a low-urgency, consultant-gated opportunity.
next_steps: Anker (owner) to screen SimplerQMS against other options, discuss budget with client, and reach out to Martin when ready — no specific date given. Martin to share meeting recording with Anker immediately.
key_quote: The current system is out of control.
loss_reason: No budget allocated; pricing may be rejected by client; early-stage evaluation with two other competitors in play and no timeline.

pain_points: The lab is stuck on an unimplemented IdeaGen/Qualtracs system with poor vendor support, relying on SharePoint and network drives for document management. Previous EQMS platforms were too pharma-centric, expensive to customize, and required costly consultant involvement for any changes.
buying_signals: Prospect's budget expectation was ~$50,000 and SimplerQMS came in at ~$31,000, generating a positive reaction to pricing. risks: State procurement rules require multiple vendor quotes, and Zen is an active competing vendor being evaluated. The clinical toxicology department is committed to MediaLab, limiting the addressable user base and potentially complicating a unified QMS rollout.

pain_points: The lab is managing ~200 SOPs and 50 work instructions across five paper copies and a Box platform, making document retrieval, versioning, and audit preparation extremely time-consuming. They want to eliminate manual administrative burden and redirect man-hours to process improvement.
buying_signals: Prospect expressed satisfaction with the overall program design and user-friendliness, has a clear go-live target of July 1st, and engaged deeply with detailed workflow and integration questions. They are actively evaluating vendors and have a defined timeline to reconvene in June.
risks: Pricing came in higher than expected and the prospect is seeking an intermediate license tier not currently offered; they are also in early-stage evaluation comparing multiple systems, with August being their busiest period creating implementation timing pressure.
next_steps: Vendor to send presentation materials, demo recording, quote, and collaboration room access immediately after call; vendor to check customer database for Thermo Sample Manager LIMS users; vendor to confirm reviewer editing capabilities via follow-up email; vendor to explore creative solutions for hands-on training tracking; prospect team to review materials and reconvene in approximately three months (June) to discuss next steps.
key_quote: Efficiency is the number one priority.

risks: A competing vendor (CaliberLIMS) offers stronger ERP/LIMS integration and is also being evaluated; SimplerQMS API capability for integrations is not available until later in 2026, which is a meaningful gap. Legal team review introduces timeline uncertainty and could delay or derail the deal.
next_steps: Martin to send updated final agreement reflecting 2-year or 3-year terms (owner: Martin, same day); Ernesto to forward vendor information and pricing to legal team today for rapid review; Ernesto and Vaibhav to discuss internally before final decision; SimplerQMS requested decision by end of Friday of the same week.
key_quote: The pricing decision is all in his hands and not his personal money.
closure_reason: C Flow Labs closed on a $15,000 SimplerQMS contract after progressing through agreement discussions that brought them to the final signing stage. The organization had clearly moved past evaluation and into implementation readiness, indicating they had validated SimplerQMS as the right eQMS solution for their life sciences operations. With agreement terms finalized and close to signature, the deal converted as the buying process reached its natural conclusion.

pain_points: Waymark manages documentation across fragmented systems (Confluence, JIRA, Google Drive) with no centralized document control, and must establish FDA-compliant QMS infrastructure to support their SAMD clearance process. They need a solution that adds compliance rigor without disrupting team velocity or existing Google-centric workflows.
buying_signals: The team engaged multiple stakeholders (co-founder, physician, paralegal, implementation lead) in the discovery call and immediately agreed to a follow-up demo with pricing for the week of March 17-21, 2026. They have an active regulatory need (FDA SAMD clearance) and have already sized their user count, signaling genuine evaluation intent.
risks: Budget is a concern as the SAMD business is framed as a growth opportunity rather than core to the company, meaning spend will be scrutinized. Waymark's heavy reliance on Google Docs and lack of native Google integration in SimplerQMS could be a deal-breaker, and they are in early-stage conversations with at least one other EQMS vendor.

pain_points: Henry Schein SA has no QMS in place; all quality and regulatory processes (document control, complaints, deviations, training) are managed manually via Excel and Teams folders by only two people, causing version control issues, compliance risks, and tasks falling through the cracks. The team urgently needs automation to reduce administrative burden and establish accountability across departments.
buying_signals: The team expressed desire to implement as soon as possible pending budget approval, and proactively requested a revised quote (2 Full, 8 Standard, 40 Light licenses) to present to the MD at a Wednesday meeting. risks: Budget approval rests with the MD who was not on the call, creating a key decision-maker gap. A local South African competitor has already been evaluated, and data center location (US/Europe) needs IT sign-off given Henry Schein's US corporate connections.
next_steps: Esme and Maricel to present revised SimplerQMS quote and business case to the MD at their Wednesday meeting; SimplerQMS to send revised quote for 2 Full, 8 Standard, and 40 Light licenses before that meeting.

pain_points: Prospect is running an outdated Lotus Notes system that functions as a document repository rather than a true EQMS, causing poor user adoption, duplicate documentation, and audit compliance issues. They need improved documentation control, corrective actions management, and integration with SAP and a future LIMS.
buying_signals: SimplerQMS is the first EQMS being formally evaluated and the prospect started searching only last week, showing early but genuine intent. They have a hard budget deadline of July 2026 to select a solution and are open to signing an agreement in October/November 2025 if payment can be deferred to January 2027.
risks: The prospect is a first-time EQMS buyer in very early evaluation stages and must present three competing software options to decision-makers, meaning SimplerQMS will face direct competition before any decision. The lack of OCR/full-text search within document bodies is a noted product gap that was left unresolved and could become a blocker.
next_steps: Follow-up demo scheduled for March 19, 2026 at 1 p.m. CET covering change management and corrective actions/non-conformities; Martin to send calendar invite. Prospect to finalize accurate user count breakdown (full, standard, and light licenses) before that call.
key_quote: If decision is not made by July, implementation will be delayed by one year.
